following items are known and either awaiting a bugfix elsewhere or, if no fix available, a workaround is suggested.

## `libarchive`: "library not loaded" or "cannot open shared object file"

### Observed errors

On Linux:

```
ImportError: libarchive.so.13: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

On macOS:

```
ImportError: dlopen(~/miniconda3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/libmambapy/bindings.cpython-311-darwin.so, 2): Library not loaded: @rpath/libarchive.13.dylib Referenced from: ~/miniconda3/lib/libmamba.2.0.0.dylib Reason: image not found
```

### Explanation

This is a known incompatibility between the `libarchive` packages published on `defaults` and `conda-forge`. The root cause is a problem in the CMake build configuration upstream (https://github.com/libarchive/libarchive/issues/1857).

### Solution

This is a packaging problem that will be eventually solved, but that won't happen until libarchive 3.8 is published (or later). For now, the recommendation is to make sure that `libarchive` and `libmamba` come from the same channel. 

### References

* https://github.com/libarchive/libarchive/issues/1857
* https://github.com/mamba-org/mamba/issues/1826
* https://github.com/conda-forge/libarchive-feedstock/issues/69
* https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/13041
* https://github.com/conda/conda-libmamba-solver/issues/244

---

## Cannot use `defaults::package` specs

### Observed errors

```
RuntimeError: The package "pkgs/main::zlib" is not available for the specified platform
```

```
InvalidMatchSpec: Invalid spec 'pkgs/main::python': This is a bug in libmamba 1.5.1 when using 'defaults::<spec>' or 'pkgs/main::<spec>'. Consider using '-c defaults' instead.
```

### Explanation

This is a known issue reported in `libmamba`: https://github.com/mamba-org/mamba/issues/2431

### Solution

Fixed in libmamba 1.5.3. Please update to the latest version.

---

## Cannot Ctrl-C out of `conda`

### Observed errors

Users are unable to use <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>C</kbd> to exit `conda` commands when `libmamba` is in use.

### Explanation

The `libmamba` C++ library has some custom signal handlers that take over the signal controllers in Python's thread. Instantiating the `libmambapy.Context` object is enough to install those handlers in the process. This prevents Python from handling `SIGINT` correctly.

### Solution

Fixed in libmamba 1.5.3. A small change is needed in `conda-libmamba-solver`, and will be made available soon.

In the meantime, users can use a different signal to kill the process. For example, `Ctrl+Z` to pause it and then issue `SIGKILL / 9` via `top` or `htop`. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/1650","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/1650/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/1650/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/1650/events","html_url":"https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/1650","id":1006482442,"node_id":"I_kwDOAgGDcc47_bQK","number":1650,"title":"LaTeX newcommand does not propagate to tex document correctly","user":{"login":"jackblyle","id":65127714,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY1MTI3NzE0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/65127714?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle","html_url":"https://github.com/jackblyle","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jackblyle/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2021-09-24T13:44:57Z","updated_at":"2022-03-07T02:04:14Z","closed_at":"2021-10-10T20:47:04Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In a jupyter notebook markdown cell I can enter a new LaTex command like:

$\newcommand{\partials}[2]{\dfrac{\partial #1}{\partial #2}}$

and then use it as:

$$\partials{L'}{q_j}$$.

In the notebook, the newcommand line will be hidden and the use of the \partials command works as expected. It displays a nice partial derivative.

BUT when I download the notebook as PDF via LaTex I get an error:

! Undefined control sequence.
l.537 \[\partials
                 {L'}{q_j}\]
? 
! Emergency stop.

I can work around the error by downloading to TeX, opening the file in an editor (say GNOME LaTeX), and manually moving the line:

\\(\newcommand{\partials}[2]{\dfrac{\partial #1}{\partial #2}}\\)

from the document body up into the preamble and deleting the unneeded \( and \). I can then save the tex file and compile to get a correct display in the PDF document. A workaround that complex isn't much of a workaround.

I have tried putting the new command up in a RawNBConvert cell at the top of the notebook without the leading and trailing $ marks. That gets the new command into the tex file at the top of the document body, right after \maketitle. But to get it to work in the notebook I have to also define it in a markdown cell, and then LaTex throws an error at the second occurrence saying Command \partials already defined.

Searching finds many versions of this question being asked (some from years ago), and sadly no working answers. Is there a trick I'm missing, or is this a true bug that has existed for years with no fix?

Running on Fedora 34.  jupyter-notebook --version  = 6.4.0.  jupyter-nbconvert -- version = 6.0.7.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060/events","html_url":"https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060","id":366561605,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUzNjY1NjE2MDU=","number":4060,"title":"Should $$..$$ in text/latex outputs be centered?","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2018-10-03T22:59:42Z","updated_at":"2022-03-08T13:37:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"See the discussion at https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/15329. Right now, outputs that are text/latex are left aligned, for instance, from SymPy: ```py from sympy import * init_printing() sqrt(3) ``` SymPy produces `$$\sqrt{3}$$`, which according to LaTeX, should be centered. Indeed, if you nbconvert the notebook to PDF, it centers it, because it just inserts the LaTeX literally. But the notebook uses CSS to force all text/latex outputs to be non-centered. At https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/15329, a change was suggested for SymPy's output to use `$\displaystyle \sqrt{3}$`, which makes no difference in the HTML notebook due to the CSS, but forces left alignment in the PDF output. I argued that nbconvert should be changed instead to left align all math outputs, regardless if they use \displaystyle or not, since nbconvert should try to produce a PDF that matches the HTML as closely as possible. However, @mgeier argues that perhaps it is more correct for the notebook to not force math to be left-aligned, and respect equation style vs. inline style, and for things that output math to choose which one to output appropriately. I would like input from the notebook devs on what they think is the appropriate fix here. Depending on which fix is decided upon, this is either a notebook issue or an nbconvert issue, but I'm opening this here for maximum visibility. Relevant issues: https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/15329 https://github.com/ipython/ipython/pull/11357 https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/pull/892 https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/275 https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/3938","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/comments/1061785528","html_url":"https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060#issuecomment-1061785528","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/4060","id":1061785528,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAgGDYc4_SY-4","user":{"login":"newptcai","id":982197,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MjE5Nw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/982197?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai","html_url":"https://github.com/newptcai","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/newptcai/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-08T13:37:06Z","updated_at":"2022-03-08T13:37:06Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Well I recently noticed this.
